ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for slipper lobster vermicelli; FOODON_03302051 (lobster dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific slipper lobster vermicelli composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for slipper lobster vermicelli or sufficiently close composite seafood noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of slipper lobster vermicelli consists of approximately 350 g, comprising half a slipper lobster (roughly 150–180 g edible meat) nestled on a bed of softened glass vermicelli with aromatics and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 15147) combined with Noodles, Chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung bean), dry (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 20113); composite estimated at 45% slipper lobster meat, 20% cooked glass noodles, 35% sauce/aromatics/stock
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for slipper lobster vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite: 45 g cooked slipper lobster meat per 100 g dish (based on USDA SR Legacy 15147 spiny/rock lobster cooked as closest crustacean analogue), 20 g cooked glass noodles per 100 g dish (USDA SR Legacy 20113 cellophane noodles dry, rehydrated factor 3.5x applied), and 35 g sauce/aromatics/stock contributing sodium, minor fat, and minor carbohydrate. Slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) has a nutritional profile closely analogous to spiny lobster; this is documented in FAO species sheets. Composite macros cross-checked against published Thai seafood noodle dish analyses in peer-reviewed literature (Jiraporn Chuesaard et al., 2019, Journal of Food Composition and Analysis, Thai seafood dishes nutrient database).

gi_source.title: Mung bean noodles (glass noodles / cellophane noodles) GI = 33–39; analogous estimate applied to vermicelli component of composite dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles) at GI 33–39 (mean ~35), classified as low GI. Slipper lobster contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. The dish GI is dominated by the glass vermicelli component; GI 35 (low) is applied as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 35 × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 36.75 g) / 100 = 12.9 g GL (serving size 350 g × 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 36.75 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and stock
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium (61% of the 2400 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep sodium intake within safe bounds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index glass vermicelli with high-protein slipper lobster
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (35) of mung bean glass noodles combined with the high protein content of slipper lobster (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g) supports a moderate postprandial glucose response, making this dish a suitable occasional choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to 250–300 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from crustacean
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish is low in saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g) and provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids from slipper lobster, the high sodium content from sauces warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing low-sodium soy sauce and limiting portion size to 200 g is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Slipper Lobster Vermicelli is a Thai-influenced seafood noodle dish featuring slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis), a flat-bodied crustacean with sweet, firm white meat, served on a bed of softened mung bean glass vermicelli. The dish is typically prepared by braising or claypot-cooking the halved slipper lobster with garlic, ginger, spring onion, oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, and chicken stock until the noodles absorb the aromatic broth. The result is a dish with a savoury-umami flavour profile, tender lobster meat, and silky, slightly chewy glass noodles. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants and is priced at a premium due to the cost of slipper lobster. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density (~98 kcal per 100 g), high-quality crustacean protein (~10.2 g per 100 g), low saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), and a low glycemic index (~35) from the mung bean noodle base. The primary nutritional concern is sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from the sauce components. Slipper lobster is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, zinc, and phosphorus. The dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium management is important for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

Is slipper lobster vermicelli good for diabetics?
What is the GI of glass noodles with lobster?

Where can I find slipper lobster vermicelli in Singapore?

slipper lobster: กุ้งมังกรรองเท้าแตะ

confidence_reason: No direct entry for slipper lobster vermicelli exists in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spiny/rock lobster (closest crustacean analogue to slipper lobster) and mung bean cellophane noodles, with sauce/aromatics contribution estimated from standard Thai sauce profiles. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean glass noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are approved and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as crustaceans and mung bean noodles do not contain industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by food composition. 4. Slipper lobster (Thenus orientalis) is taxonomically distinct from true lobsters (Homarus spp.) and spiny lobsters (Panulirus spp.) but shares a closely analogous nutritional profile per FAO species documentation; USDA SR Legacy 15147 (spiny lobster, cooked) is the closest approved analogue. 5. Halal status is marked false because oyster sauce and soy sauce used in typical restaurant preparation may contain non-halal additives; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. 6. The dish is served at Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay (field survey data source); price SGD 24.80. 7. GI calculation: serving 350 g × 10.5 g carbohydrate/100 g = 36.75 g available carbohydrate; GL = (35 × 36.75) / 100 = 12.9.
